videoTitle: I'm in DevOps - Deep Dive: Infrastructure as Code on AWS (Level 300)
videoDescription:
Incorporating infrastructure as code into software development practices can help teams and organizations improve automation and throughput without sacrificing quality and uptime. In this session, we cover infrastructure as code on AWS with AWS CloudFormation and the AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK). We discuss best practices for writing, testing, and maintaining template code, giving you the opportunity to learn about IDE plugins, reusability, testing tools, modularizing stacks, and more. Finally, we review sample code that showcases some of the best practices for more context and clarity.

Darko is a Senior Developer Advocate focusing on Central and Eastern Europe. He shares his passion and technological know-how with Engineers, Developers, and communities across the world. If it can be automated, Darko tries to do so. Most of his focus is towards DevOps and Management Tools, where automation, pipelines, and efficient developer tools is the name of the game – click less and code more so you do not repeat yourself - is what he says!  He has a long IT career in various areas, from retail, to government, to even large telcos. At AWS, Darko has worked as a Premium Support Engineer and as a Solutions Architect, which has given him first-hand experience with AWS customers and their AWS adventures, helping them learn the ways of the cloud!  As a passionate collector of old computers, he has a collection of computers and games and they came as an inspiration to tinker with computers as much as possible! Darko comes from Serbia, but has lived in Czech Republic, Malta, Ireland and now Germany!

인포그랩은 GitLab의 한국 최고의 파트너 이며 Agile 과 DevOps 서비스 회사 입니다.
경험 있는 전문 컨설턴트, 엔지니어들이 고객의 다음 니즈를 해결해 드립니다.

- GitLab 라이센스 비용 절감
- ITSM 고도화, DevSecOps 구축 및 개선 (진단→개선→실행→진단)
- 쿠버네티스(k8s) 구축 및 DevOps 파이프라인 개선 (Helm Chart 운영 지원)
- 맞춤 설치와 환경 설정 혹은 최신 버전으로 업그레이드
- CI/CD 파이프라인을 구축하거나, 관련 기능 커스텀 개발
- Git, GitLab에 교육 제공, 개발 팀의 협업 개선을 위한 워크숍 진행
- Jira, Jenkins, SonarQube, Artifactory, Nexus, DevSecOps Tool 연동 및 통합 또는 마이그레이션
- SVN, Perforce, CSV, ClearCase, BitBucket, GitHub, SourceSafe를  Git&GitLab으로 전환

In this episode, Microsoft Technical Trainer Andrew Conniff introduces Module 01: Introduction to DevOps. You’ll explore DevOps as the union of people, process, and products to enable continuous delivery of value, and learn why continuous improvement and cycle-time optimization are essential in modern software delivery.

The session covers the fundamentals of Git repositories, branching workflows, pull requests, collaboration, and code review. You’ll also examine key DevOps practices, including CI/CD, monitoring and logging, cloud fundamentals, infrastructure as code, microservices, and containers. The episode concludes with an overview of Azure DevOps (Boards, Pipelines, Repos, Artifacts, Test Plans) and GitHub (Repositories, Codespaces, Actions, Packages, and security features) and how they support end-to-end DevOps workflows aligned to AZ-400 certification objectives.

Learn more about this course and take the certification exam to test your new skills: https://aka.ms/AZ-400onLearn

videoTitle: Build an AI PR Reviewer with GitHub Actions (DevOps Tutorial - AWS Bedrock + Terraform)
videoDescription:
0:00 Intro – Why DevOps Needs AI Now
0:40 Demo – AI Reviewing a Pull Request with AWS Bedrock
1:08 Environment Setup (Terraform, AWS, GitHub, VS Code)
1:55 Creating the GitHub Repo with Terraform
3:12 Terraform Init & First Apply
3:53 Project Structure (infra/ + scripts/)
6:21 Creating S3 Backend & DynamoDB State Lock
8:21 Setting Up GitHub OIDC in AWS IAM
11:39 Terraform Plan & Apply (IAM + Bedrock Role)
12:26 Building the GitHub Actions Workflow
15:42 Writing the Python Bedrock Review Script
20:51 Testing the PR Workflow
21:58 Fixing “No Merge Base” GitHub Error
23:34 Debugging Bedrock Model Issues (Claude EOL)
24:40 Switching to Nova 2 Light Model
25:44 Manual Bedrock API Fix (Inference Config)
30:28 Improving Human-Readable Output
33:31 Successful AI PR Review Demo
33:50 Testing AI Against Insecure EC2 Example
35:50 AI Security Findings & Best Practice Feedback
36:47 Final Thoughts – AI Won’t Replace You (But You Must Adapt)

In this video, I build an AI-powered DevOps pull request reviewer using GitHub Actions, AWS Bedrock, and Terraform.

Instead of relying on manual code reviews, this pipeline automatically:

Assumes an AWS IAM role using OIDC (no static keys)

Extracts the pull request diff

Sends the changes to an AI model in AWS Bedrock

Posts a structured DevOps review directly on the PR

We’ll cover:

Terraform remote state best practices

GitHub OIDC federation with AWS

IAM trust policies

Bedrock model invocation

Automating infrastructure reviews with AI

This is what modern AI-integrated DevOps pipelines look like.

If you're a DevOps engineer, platform engineer, or cloud engineer looking to integrate AI into CI/CD, this build will give you a practical starting point.

🔔 Subscribe for more AI + DevOps + Terraform content
💬 Comment if you'd like to see this expanded into production-grade policy enforcement
🚀 Next: Blocking merges based on AI risk scoring

videoTitle: What is the Git Index? || #Git #GitHub #GitLab #DevOps #JavaScript #Python #Java #Bitbucket #GitGut
videoDescription:
Ever wonder what the Git index is and why we must use the git staging index and stage a file before we perform a commit?

Well, Git isn't like Mircosoft Word's autosave feature. You have to tell it which files to include in the next Git commit. That's called staging a file in Git.

So yes, you have to stage files first, and when you're happy with your code, you perform the Git commit, and all the staged files will become part of your Git commit history.

You can learn more about staging a Git file and the git add command here:

https://www.theserverside.com/blog/Coffee-Talk-Java-News-Stories-and-Opinions/git-add-index-stage-file-staging-commit-combine-untracked-staging-status

videoTitle: Real world DevOps project from start to finish - WireGuard VPN Part 1
videoDescription:
CHECK OUT MY DEVOPS COURSE WITH 85% OFF COUPON AT https://sre.rs

Hello everyone,


I've made a DevOps course covering a lot of different technologies and applications,
aimed at startups, small companies and individuals who want to self-host
their infrastructure.

This video shows the first part of setting up WireGuard VPN.

TL;DR:
Course page: https://www.udemy.com/course/real-world-devops-project-from-start-to-finish/?referralCode=9AC467077593D22878A2
Discount coupon is available at https://sre.rs

Details:
To get this out of the way - this course doesn't cover Kubernetes or similar -
I'm of the opinion that for startups, small companies, and especially individuals,
you probably don't need Kubernetes. Unless you have a whole DevOps team,
it usually brings more problems than benefits, and unnecessary infrastructure bills
buried a lot of startups before they got anywhere.

As for prerequisites, you can't be a complete beginner in the world of computers.
If you've never even heard of Docker, if you don't know at least something about DNS,
or if you don't have any experience with Linux, this course is probably not for you.
That being said, I do explain the basics too, but probably not in enough detail
for a complete beginner.

I encourage you to watch "free preview" videos to get the sense of what
will be covered, but here's the gist:

The goal of the course is to create an easily deployable and reproducible server
which will have "everything" a startup or a small company will need - VPN, mail, Git,
CI/CD, messaging, hosting websites and services, sharing files, calendar, etc.
It can also be useful to individuals who want to self-host all of those - I ditched Google 99.9%
and other than that being a good feeling, I'm not worried that some AI bug will lock my account
with no one to talk to about resolving the issue.

Considering that it covers a wide variety of topics, it doesn't go in depth in any of those.
Think of it as going down a highway towards the end destination, but on the way there I show you
all the junctions where I think it's useful to do more research on the subject.

We'll deploy services inside Docker and LXC (Linux Containers). Those will include
a mail server (iRedMail), Zulip (Slack and Microsoft Teams alternative), GitLab (with GitLab Runner and CI/CD),
Nextcloud (file sharing, calendar, contacts, etc.), checkmk (monitoring solution), Pi-hole (ad blocking on DNS level),
Traefik with Docker and file providers (a single HTTP/S entry point with automatic routing and TLS certificates).

We'll set up WireGuard, a modern and fast VPN solution for secure access to VPS' internal network,
and I'll also show you how to get a wildcard TLS certificate with certbot and DNS provider.

To wrap it all up, we'll write a simple Python application that will compare a list of the desired backups
with the list of finished backups, and send a result to a Zulip stream.
We'll write the application, do a 'git push' to GitLab which will trigger a CI/CD pipeline
that will build a Docker image, push it to a private registry, and then,
with the help of the GitLab runner, run it on the VPS and post a result to a Zulip stream with a webhook.


If you apply the coupon, I'd appreciate if you leave your feedback on Udemy
after you're done and spread the word about the course. It would mean a lot.


I hope that you find it useful.


Happy learning,
Predrag

videoTitle: Master IaC with Azure DevOps (C# + Azure Infrastructure Guide) | Workshop
videoDescription:
Learn how to automate Azure infrastructure with Azure DevOps and Pulumi — using C# and Infrastructure as Code (IaC).

This hands-on workshop walks you through setting up an Azure DevOps pipeline that automatically provisions real Azure resources with Pulumi, a modern Infrastructure as Code platform. You’ll use C# to define, deploy, and manage infrastructure safely — no ARM templates or portal clicks required.

By the end, you’ll understand how to connect Azure DevOps with Pulumi, apply Infrastructure as Code best practices, and continuously deliver cloud infrastructure through a real DevOps workflow.

▬▬▬ Workshop Prerequisites ▬▬▬
► Sign up for a Free  Pulumi account: https://pulumip.us/Free-Sign-Up

👨‍💻Demo Code: https://github.com/pulumi/workshops/tree/main/az-csharp-app

▬▬▬ What You’ll Learn ▬▬▬
✅ Build and configure a full Azure DevOps pipeline that deploys real Azure infrastructure
✅ Use C# and Pulumi to manage cloud infrastructure as code
✅ Automate provisioning — no manual portal steps or ARM templates
✅ Preview and approve every change before deployment
✅ Commit, push, and let your cloud update itself automatically

▬▬▬ 📌 Resources ▬▬▬
🎓 Register for upcoming workshops: https://pulumip.us/Upcoming-Workshops
🚀 Get Started with Pulumi: https://pulumip.us/Get-Started_Azure
🤖 Try Pulumi Neo: https://www.pulumi.com/product/neo/
💬 Talk with us: https://slack.pulumi.com/
channelDescription:
Pulumi is an open-source infrastructure-as-code platform that lets you build, deploy, and manage cloud infrastructure with programming languages like TypeScript, Python, Go, C#, and Java, instead of domain-specific languages like HCL or YAML.

With Pulumi, you can bring everything your chosen language has to offer — loops, conditionals, type checking, error handling, test frameworks, package ecosystems, and more — to the infrastructure domain. And because it’s real code, AI agents love it as much as humans do.

videoTitle: I Turned Claude Code Into My DevOps For OpenClaw
videoDescription:
I wanted OpenClaw on my VPS without having to babysit anything, or care about service restart, config, gateway issus, and upgrades  myself.

So I built an OpenClaw DevOps Kit that gives Claude Code the playbooks it needs to be a DevOps operator on the server. It can bootstrap the VPS, install Claude Code if needed, bring in the OpenClaw playbooks, install and configure OpenClaw, set up alerts, help with security checks, keep an eye on the gateway, and repair things when needed.

The watchdog repo is open source and contains the core functionality that does most of the fuctionality. My personal, customzied version with the broader setup flow that I personally like and extra playbooks is on Ko-Fi.

https://ko-fi.com/s/8752f1ccc7

videoTitle: The "Hidden" Linux Memory Leak 🐧 #devops #linux #bash
videoDescription:
Stop blaming your application for every memory leak! 🛑

Sometimes the Linux Kernel is the one hoarding your RAM. In this video, we use slabtop to uncover "Invisible" memory consumption caused by Dentry and Inode caches.

Commands used in this video: 1️⃣ grep SUnreclaim /proc/meminfo — See if the Kernel is locking RAM. 2️⃣ sudo slabtop -o -s c — Find the specific metadata hog. 3️⃣ echo 2 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches — Force a reclaim.

The Real Solution: Tuning vm.vfs_cache_pressure is the senior move to prevent this from happening in production.

🧠 Want to find leaks inside your ACTUAL code? Watch my deep dive on User-Space memory leaks here: [LINK TO YOUR LONG VIDEO]

videoTitle: Bash Scripting for DevOps Engineers | Automate Kubernetes, Disk & Log Monitoring
videoDescription:
Bash scripting is one of the most powerful skills every DevOps engineer should know.

In real DevOps environments, engineers repeatedly check things like:
• Is the Kubernetes pod running?
• Is the disk almost full?
• Are there errors in system logs?

Doing this manually once is fine — but doing it every day is not scalable.

This is where bash scripting helps automate repetitive operational tasks.

In this tutorial, you’ll learn the fundamentals of shell scripting and then apply them to real DevOps automation examples used in production environments.

🔧 What You’ll Learn

• How to create and execute a shell script
• Understanding the shebang (#!/bin/bash)
• Setting executable permissions with chmod +x
• Using variables in bash scripting
• Writing if-else conditions for automation decisions
• Using for loops in shell scripts
• Using exit codes for automation workflows

🚀 Real DevOps Automation Scripts

✔ Kubernetes Pod Health Check Script (kubectl + JSONPath)
✔ Disk Usage Monitoring Script with threshold alerts (df, awk, sed)
✔ Real-time Log Monitoring Script using tail -f and grep

These examples demonstrate how small scripts can automate daily DevOps operations like monitoring services, detecting failures, and preventing production issues.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why DevOps Engineers Automate Repetitive Tasks
00:34 - How to Create and Run Your First Shell Script
02:15 - Using Variables in Shell Scripts
03:26 - If Conditions in Bash (Automation Decisions)
06:48 - For Loops in Shell Scripts
08:58 - From Concepts to Real DevOps Automation
09:30 - Script #1: Check Kubernetes Pod Health
12:35 - Script #2: Monitor Disk Usage Automatically
16:09 - Script #3: Detect Errors in Log Files
18:44 - How Small Scripts Power DevOps Automation
19:06 - DevOps Mindset: When to Automate Commands

📌 If you're learning DevOps, Kubernetes, or Linux automation, mastering bash scripting is one of the most valuable skills you can develop.

videoTitle: 5 Core SRE Topics Every Interviewer Will Ask About #devops #sre
videoDescription:
If you're preparing for an SRE interview, here are 5 core topics you must master:

First — SLI, SLO, SLA.
Understand how reliability is actually measured in production. Not definitions — real metrics, error budgets, and what they mean during incidents.
https://youtu.be/CleIOnYMl1Y

Second — Distributed Tracing with Prometheus, Grafana, Tempo, and OTEL.
Learn how a request flows across services. This is what interviewers expect when they ask about microservices debugging.
https://youtu.be/MUX4lYBdmQU

Third — Metrics with Prometheus and OpenTelemetry.
Know how your application exposes data — CPU, memory, request latency — and how you visualize it in Grafana.
https://youtu.be/B_muisnbf78

Fourth — Centralized Logging with Loki.
Logs are your ground truth. Learn how to aggregate and query logs across services during failures.
https://youtu.be/sCQcvGrsv_E

Fifth — Production Monitoring for a Spring Boot App.
Put everything together — metrics, logs, tracing — full observability stack like real SREs use.
https://youtu.be/k7bzxk1QtF8

If you can explain and demo these, you're already ahead of most candidates.

videoTitle: DevOps Realnosti: Dobre i loše strane koje niko ne pominje
videoDescription:
DevOps je postao standard u modernom IT svetu, ali često se zaboravlja da ova metodologija dolazi sa sopstvenim setom prednosti i izazova. U ovom videu Katarina i Gorana analiziraju DevOps kroz prizmu realnih projekata, bez ulepšavanja i teorijskih definicija.

Govorimo o onome što DevOps zaista donosi kada se primeni u produkciji. S jedne strane imamo povećanu brzinu isporuke softvera, bolju saradnju timova i stabilnost sistema, ali s druge strane postoji kompleksnost, visoki zahtevi za znanjem i otpor prema promenama kulture unutar firme.

Ovaj razgovor je namenjen svima koji žele da razumeju šta zaista znači usvojiti DevOps kulturu. Bilo da ste developer, sistemski administrator ili menadžer koji odlučuje o tehnološkom pravcu kompanije, važno je znati gde su granice automatizacije i gde počinju ljudski i organizacioni izazovi. Delimo lekcije koje smo naučile radeći na kompleksnim sistemima i objašnjavamo zašto DevOps za neke timove postaje prednost, dok za druge predstavlja nepotrebnu komplikaciju.

videoTitle: The Bear that Dreams of DevOps

videoTitle: Zero Downtime Deployment Explained | Blue-Green Strategy in DevOps (Day 4)
videoDescription:

“How do you deploy a high-traffic application with zero downtime?”

✅ What Blue-Green Deployment is

videoTitle: Why DevOps Engineers Should Learn AI Infrastructure
videoDescription:
Why DevOps Engineers Should Learn AI Infrastructure (MLOps, AI Agents & Kubernetes)

Most DevOps engineers today are learning Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability, cloud platforms, and platform engineering.

But another major shift is happening underneath all of it: AI Infrastructure.

Behind every ChatGPT response, AI Agent, RAG application, recommendation engine, and LLM-powered system is a complex infrastructure stack involving GPUs, Kubernetes, model serving, inference systems, vector databases, observability, and distributed systems.

The interesting part?
Many of the skills required to build and operate these systems already overlap with what DevOps engineers have been doing for years.

In this video, you'll learn:

✅ Why AI Infrastructure is growing so rapidly
✅ Training vs Inference explained
✅ Why inference is the real production workload
✅ GPUs and Kubernetes for AI workloads
✅ Model Serving and AI Platforms
✅ RAG, Embeddings & Vector Databases
✅ AI Observability and Monitoring
✅ AI Agents as Distributed Systems
✅ ML Engineer vs MLOps vs AI Infrastructure Engineer vs Platform Engineer
✅ Why DevOps Engineers are uniquely positioned for this opportunity

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why DevOps Engineers Should Care About AI Infrastructure
00:56 - AI Changed Infrastructure Forever
01:48 - Training vs Inference (What Actually Runs in Production)
03:16 - GPUs, Kubernetes & AI Workloads
04:25 - How Kubernetes Serves AI Models
05:28 - Why DevOps Engineers Have an Advantage
05:50 - RAG, Embeddings & Vector Databases Explained
07:04 - Why AI Observability Is Hard
07:46 - AI Agents Are Distributed Systems
09:16 - ML Engineer vs MLOps vs AI Infra vs Platform Engineer
11:06 - The AI Infrastructure Opportunity for DevOps Engineers

Series: From DevOps to AI Engineering

In this series, we'll explore AI from a systems engineering perspective, including:

• AI Infrastructure
• MLOps & LLMOps
• AI Agents
• Kubernetes for AI
• GPUs & Model Serving
• Vector Databases

If you're a DevOps, Platform, SRE, Cloud, or Infrastructure Engineer looking to understand where AI is heading, this series is for you.

videoTitle: Stop using Read-Host and do this instead #powershell  #devops  #scripting
videoDescription:
Stop using Read-Host and do this instead:

Read-Host has limitations. 

- You cannot pass information to it 
- You cannot validate what the user provided
- It always stops the script

There is a better way - use parameters instead.

- Control the type of input
- Make it mandatory or optional
- Pass argument from the command line
- or prompt the user to provide an argument, when required and not passed

In this short video, I'll show you how to implement simple parameters and how to use them.

Would you like to learn more about the parameters?

videoTitle: Improving DevOps Security and Efficiency at Cathay with AWS ProServe | Amazon Web Services
videoDescription:
Cathay set out to better understand how cybersecurity was integrated across its development processes. With nearly 1,000 developers working across global teams, false positives and late-stage fixes were slowing innovation and increasing costs.

Working with AWS ProServe, Cathay assessed and modernized its DevOps workflows using AI-powered tools. The result was earlier detection of vulnerabilities, streamlined remediation, and more efficient development cycles—reducing costs by up to 80% and improving developer experience.

videoTitle: Day 25 – Infrastructure as Code (IaC) in Azure DevOps | 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge
videoDescription:
🚀 Welcome to Day 25 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge!

🔹 Today’s topic: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

In this session, I’ll explain what IaC is and why it’s a game-changer in the world of DevOps. Instead of manually configuring servers and networks, IaC allows us to define infrastructure in code and deploy it quickly, consistently, and at scale.

✅ You’ll learn:

What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?

Why IaC saves time and avoids errors

Popular tools like Terraform, ARM templates, and Bicep

Real-life analogy: IaC is like following a recipe 🧑‍🍳 for consistent results

✨ Key takeaway: Infrastructure as Code = Speed, Consistency, and Automation

📌 Next up in Day 26 → We’ll dive deeper into Terraform with Azure DevOps to see IaC in action.

👉 Don’t forget to like 👍, share 🔁, and subscribe 🔔 for more practical DevOps learning!

#AzureDevOps #IaC #Terraform #DevOps #CloudComputing #30DaysChallenge #LearningJourney

videoTitle: Docker Architecture Explained | Docker Client, Host & Registry | DevOps Tutorial
videoDescription:
Learn Docker Architecture step-by-step and understand how Docker Client, Docker Host, and Docker Registry work together to run containers.

In this video, we will understand Docker Architecture in detail.

Before running Docker containers or deploying microservices, it is important to understand how Docker works internally. In this tutorial, we will explain the core components of Docker architecture including Docker Client, Docker Host (Docker Daemon), Docker Images, Docker Containers, and Docker Registry.

We will also understand how Docker commands work internally and how Docker pulls images from Docker Hub to create containers.

Topics Covered:
✔ Docker Client
✔ Docker Host
✔ Docker Daemon
✔ Docker Images
✔ Docker Containers
✔ Docker Registry
✔ Docker Hub
✔ Real-world Docker workflow

This video is part of the Code2CloudX DevOps learning series where we move step by step from basic infrastructure concepts to containerization and cloud deployment.

In the next video, we will install Docker and create our first Docker container.

Subscribe to Code2CloudX to learn:
• DevOps

• Cloud deployment

Your tests are in TestRail. Your work items are in ADO. Your team is losing time in between.

This time we invited our certified platinum partner @merito_solutions to bring common field problems caused by disconnected QA and dev workflows—manual exports, screenshots, shadow spreadsheets, unreliable dashboards, and delayed reporting—along with resulting release risk and compliance burdens.

- The Cost of the Gap: How disconnected QA and dev workflows create missed defects, manual status chasing, and incomplete release decisions

videoTitle: Senior DevOps Nightmares (Part 2/2): The Deep-Dives of 49 Outages That Keep Staff Engineers Awake !!
videoDescription:
-📌 Watch Part 1 (Platform & CI/CD Failures):  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FucGmd2EK00

This is Part 2 of the DevOps Nightmares series.

In Part 1, we covered platform, CI/CD, and Kubernetes failures.

In this video, we go deeper—into the failures that happen below your application and beyond your tools.

In Part 2 of this Reference-Grade Masterclass, we leave the world of simple pipelines behind and descend into the Ghost in the Machine. We are diving deep into the 49 most complex, systemic, and kernel-level failures that separate a Senior Operator from a Staff Systems Architect.

We continue using our 9-step D.E.B.U.G. framework to perform forensic analysis on Category 9 through 16, covering:
-The Kernel Abyss: conntrack exhaustion, eBPF map limits, and CNI IP famines.
-Stateful Survival: Postgres TXID wraparounds, HA Split-brain, and Shard Skew.
-Distributed Theory: Kafka rebalance storms, Idempotency disasters, and Raft consensus loss.
-High-Scale Performance: The Linux CFS Quota trap and Cache Stampedes.
-Cloud Economics: Cross-AZ cost explosions and Regional Gray Failures.
-The Identity Perimeter: IAM role chaining, OIDC expirations, and Lateral Movement.
-Fleet-Wide Ops: xDS cache desync and global mTLS rotation storms.

Every scenario includes a Mechanism Deep Dive, Scale Trade-off Analysis, and a Staff-Level Interview Curveball to test your engineering intuition.

Chapters:
 - The Ghost in the Machine: Part 2 Intro
 - Part 1 Recap & Volume 2 Roadmap
 - Category 9: Advanced Networking (SNAT, DNS, N+1)
 - Category 10: The Kernel Abyss (conntrack, eBPF, CNI)
 - Category 11: Stateful Survival (Postgres, HA, EBS IOPS)
 - Category 12: Distributed Messaging (Kafka, Raft, Idempotency)
- Category 13: High-Scale Performance (CFS Quotas, Stampedes)
 - Category 14: Cloud Economics (Cross-AZ Costs, Gray Failures)
 - Category 15: Identity & Security (OIDC, IAM, Exfiltration)
 - Category 16: Multi-Cluster & Chaos (xDS, mTLS, Fault Injection)
 - Final Conclusion: The Senior Mindset
---

These are the incidents where:

* Your dashboards look fine
* Your services are “healthy”
* But the system is already failing underneath
---

⚠️ Topics include:

* conntrack exhaustion & SNAT limits
* Kafka backlog storms & retry cascades
* Postgres TXID wraparound & split-brain
* Cache stampedes & cold-start floods
* Cloud rate limits & cross-AZ cost explosions
* IAM failures & secret rotation crashes
---

🎯 If you are:

* Preparing for Senior / Staff DevOps roles
* Designing large-scale distributed systems
* Running production systems at scale

This video will change how you debug.
---

👍 Like, Subscribe & Save this series
Because these are the failures you face when systems scale.

videoTitle: AI Agent to Monitor Azure DevOps Comments (Google ADK + MCP Tutorial) #aiagent #mcp #azure #devops
videoDescription:
Learn how to build an AI agent that automatically monitors Azure DevOps work item comments and sends daily email summaries using Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) and Model Context Protocol (MCP).

In this tutorial, you'll learn how to:
• Set up Google ADK agent
• Integrate it with Azure DevOps MCP server
• Build an agent to monitor work item comments for mentions

videoTitle: Day 23 – Docker with Azure Pipelines | Azure DevOps 30 Days Challenge
videoDescription:
Welcome to Day 23 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀

Today’s focus: Docker with Azure Pipelines

🔹 What is Docker?
Think of Docker as a box that contains everything your app needs – code, dependencies, and environment – so it runs the same anywhere.

🔹 What is Azure Pipelines?
Azure Pipelines is the delivery system that builds that box, tests it, and ships it to where it needs to run.

Together, Docker + Azure Pipelines solve the famous “it works on my machine” problem and make deployments fast, reliable, and consistent across environments.

👉 Key takeaway:

Docker = the box for your app 🐳

Azure Pipelines = the delivery system for that box 🚚

📌 Next in Day 24 → we’ll connect Docker with Kubernetes to see how containers run at scale.

✅ Watch the full 30 Days of Azure DevOps Playlist here: [paste playlist link]

Jira is a foundational system of record for agile development, IT service management, and operational workflows. In this demo, see how Commvault Cloud Backup and Recovery for DevOps delivers enterprise-grade protection for Jira—helping teams safeguard work items, spaces, and configurations against accidental deletion, misconfiguration, outages, and cyber threats.

* Frequency and schedule
* Retention (including long-term or indefinite retention)

* Automatic and on-demand backups (full, incremental, synthetic)
* Recovery Calendar for point-in-time restores
* Flexible restore options:
* Full Jira site recovery
* Individual space recovery
* In-place or out-of-place restore
* Audit logs and reporting for governance and compliance

Key Benefits
* Reduce risk of Jira data loss or corruption
* Minimize disruption to development and IT workflows
* Support compliance and internal governance requirements
* Enable disaster recovery and migration scenarios
* Protect Jira as part of unified DevOps resilience

TL;DR
This demo shows how Commvault Cloud protects Jira data with automated backups, flexible recovery options, and audit-ready visibility for DevOps teams.

Who Is This For?
* CIOs and IT Leaders
* DevOps and Engineering Teams
* Jira Administrators
* IT Service Management (ITSM) Teams
* Security and Compliance Leaders
* SaaS Backup and Platform Teams

FAQ
Does Commvault support Jira Cloud?
Yes. Commvault Cloud Backup and Recovery supports protection and recovery for Jira environments using secure OAuth integration.

Can I restore individual spaces or work items?
Yes. You can restore entire Jira sites or individual spaces, depending on your recovery needs.

Are backups automated?
Yes. Backups run automatically once configured, with options for on-demand full, incremental, or synthetic backups.

Does Commvault support long-term retention?
Yes. Retention policies can be configured for defined timeframes, including long-term or indefinite retention.

Is audit logging included?
Yes. Backup and recovery activities are captured through detailed audit logs to support compliance and governance requirements.

videoTitle: How to Securely Store Passwords and API Keys | DevOps Secrets Management (Day 6)
videoDescription:
🔐 Day 6 of the DevOps in ARIA – Real-Time Scenario Series!

Today’s Scenario:
How do you securely store and manage sensitive information like passwords, API keys, and tokens?

In this video, I share real-world best practices that every DevOps engineer must follow to protect secrets and avoid security breaches.

Here’s what we cover:

Why you should never hard-code secrets (even in private repos)

How to use secret management tools like AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault, Kubernetes Secrets

Why the principle of least privilege matters

How and when to rotate secrets securely

Best ways to inject secrets at runtime using environment variables or secret mounts

This topic isn’t just for interviews — it’s critical for real-world security.

Takeaway:
Mastering secret management builds trust, safety, and reliability into your DevOps pipeline.

videoTitle: DevOps vs SRE Explained | What's the difference?
videoDescription:
In this video, I break down the key differences between DevOps and Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), two essential approaches to modern infrastructure management. While both aim to improve software delivery and system reliability, their goals, methods, and roles are distinct.

Key Takeaways:
- DevOps: Focuses on collaboration between development and operations teams to enhance speed, automation, and deployment efficiency.
- SRE: Prioritizes system reliability, performance, and accountability through software engineering principles and metrics like SLOs and error budgets.
- Real-World Applications: How these concepts are applied in various organizations, and how DevOps and SRE can complement each other.

#itcareer #realtech #technology #devops #devopslife #devopsculture #devopsengineer #sre #sitereliabilityengineering

videoTitle: Evig Eies | DNA-DevOps | The Song of the Infinite Cycle of Digital Creation and Evolution
videoDescription:
Welcome to the sonic universe of "Evig Eies", the track that dives into the poetic intersection of code, creation, and human nature.

Inspired by the concept of DNA-DevOps—the fundamental, organic rhythm that pulses beneath all software engineering and innovation—this song is a reflection on the Infinite Cycle of Creation, Refinement, and Lasting Ownership.

🌌 The Essence of the Music:
"Evig Eies" (which translates to "Forever/Eternally Owned" or "Eternal Property") is our declaration that the knowledge, the code, and the bridges we build digitally are inseparable parts of who we are.

The track moves from a sparse and reflective start (Piano and Cello) to a subtly driving rhythm (Bass and Drums), mirroring a project's journey: from the "Concept's spark" to a "Living stream".

It’s an homage to the "Silent promise" present in every line of code and the relentless pursuit to refine, adapt, and "conquer fear" in the tech environment.

🎼 Who is this song for?
For software engineers, developers, IT professionals, digital artists, and anyone who finds beauty and purpose in the cycle of continuous creation and improvement. It's the soundtrack for late-night coding sessions, for learning, and for the union between logic and emotion.

Leave a comment and tell us which part of the lyrics resonated most with you! Do you feel this DNA-DevOps in your daily routine?

(Evig Eies, forever owned, forever known...)

Verse 1:
(Piano and Cello begin, sparse and reflective)
Lines of code, a digital thread,
Weaving futures, words unsaid.
From concept's spark to a living stream,
A silent promise, a waking dream.
Chorus:
(Bass and drums enter, subtle and driving, clean electric guitar adds texture)
Oh, DNA-DevOps, a rhythm deep inside,
Where logic flows and errors hide.
Evig Eies, a truth we hold so dear,
Building bridges, conquering fear.
Verse 2:
(Vocals become more prominent, velvety and slightly melancholic)
Through countless iterations, we refine,
Each commit, a purpose, a design.
From whispered thoughts to a grand display,
Guiding progress, lighting the way.
Chorus:
(Instrumentation builds slightly, still restrained)
Oh, DNA-DevOps, a rhythm deep inside,
Where logic flows and errors hide.
Evig Eies, a truth we hold so dear,
Building bridges, conquering fear.
Bridge:
(Cello takes a more melodic lead, piano arpeggios, drums become more intricate)
In the quiet hum of the server's grace,
A human touch in this digital space.
We learn, we grow, we adapt, we mend,
A cycle endless, without an end.
Chorus:
(Fuller sound, but still maintaining the minimalist feel)
Oh, DNA-DevOps, a rhythm deep inside,
Where logic flows and errors hide.
Evig Eies, a truth we hold so dear,
Building bridges, conquering fear.
Outro:
(Instrumentation slowly fades, piano and cello linger)
Evig Eies, forever owned, forever known...
A silent promise, deeply sown.
(Silence)

#DNADevOps #EvigEies #CodingMusic #SoftwareEngineering #DevOps #IndieMusic #AmbientPop #TechMusic #CreationCycle #originalmusic

videoTitle: Master Cloud Computing Autoscaling: Save Money and Boost Performance! #devops #cloud #autoscaling
videoDescription:
Autoscaling is a game-changer in cloud computing, allowing you to dynamically adjust computational resources based on the load on your server farm. This means you can automatically increase or decrease the number of servers running behind your web application, optimizing for both performance and cost efficiency. 💡

00:00 Introduction

03:46 Question 2: How to Check Running Processes in Linux

16:28 Question 9: What Is DNS?

17:44 Question 10: How Do You Use ChatGPT or AI Tools at Work?

18:54 Linux Interview Tips & Troubleshooting Mindset

---

## 💻 COMMANDS USED IN THIS VIDEO

### Check CPU Usage

top

htop

### Check Memory Usage

free -m

### Check Disk Usage

df -h

### View System Logs

journalctl -xe

### List Running Processes

ps aux

### Monitor Processes in Real Time

top

### Top Memory Consumers

ps aux --sort=-%mem | head

### Top CPU Consumers

ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head

### Graceful Process Termination

kill PID

### Force Kill Process

kill -9 PID

### Linux Permissions

chmod 755 script.sh

### Check Open Ports

ss -tulnp

netstat -tulnp

### Check Nginx Status

systemctl status nginx

### Check Nginx Logs

journalctl -u nginx

### Test Website Locally

curl localhost

### Docker Commands

docker ps

docker images

### DNS Troubleshooting

dig google.com

nslookup google.com

---

channelName: The Engineering Wisdom | Rakia Ben Sassi
videoTitle: Cold Starts Explained: The Hidden Challenge of Serverless Computing #serverlesscomputing #devops
videoDescription:
In a serverless environment, functions run on-demand as a response to a triggered event, like a user request. Since the initialization of a function instance takes some time, this approach causes what we call startup latency and “cold starts.”

The startup latency can vary significantly — it may range from a few milliseconds to several seconds and it depends on different factors — many of them under the developer’s control.

🎥  Watch the complete video here: https://youtu.be/rA1b-9G1dXw

💡 Read the written version here: https://medium.com/better-programming/serverless-bcc0d2b65b41?sk=cfb4227bdd1ac1827d593b86563e5b1d

videoTitle: Kubernetes High Availability Made Easy #kubernetes #devops
videoDescription:

In this video, I break down Kubernetes High Availability (HA) — including the control plane, multiple masters, etcd leader & followers, and how a load balancer keeps your cluster fault-tolerant.

- How etcd handles leader and follower replicas
- Why controller-manager & scheduler run in standby mode
- How HA works with multiple masters and a load balancer

This is the foundation of reliability in production Kubernetes clusters.

videoTitle: DSI Seminar | Transforming DevOps for Scientific Innovation
videoDescription:
Over the last decade, the Materials Project (MP, materialsproject.org) has evolved from a niche research database into the world's leading open-access platform for materials data, now serving a rapidly growing community of over 700,000 registered users. Supporting this exponential growth while managing over hundreds of terabytes of scientific data required a fundamental architectural shift from on-premises hardware to a resilient, cloud-native ecosystem. In this session, Dr. Huck detailed the engineering strategy behind MP’s modernization. He provided a technical “under-the-hood” look at how his team leverages AWS CloudFormation for Infrastructure-as-Code; utilizes Kong API Gateway to orchestrate traffic across containerized microservices; and achieves end-to-end observability with Datadog. The talk covered practical lessons in scaling scientific infrastructure, including the implementation of blue/green deployments for backend services and the strategic use of the AWS Open Data Program to democratize access to materials data.

Dr. Patrick Huck is a Senior Computing Engineer (CSE5) at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, where he serves as the Technical Lead for the Materials Project. He is the chief platform architect behind what is widely known as the “Google of Materials”—a massive open-access platform that harnesses supercomputing to predict material properties, helping over 700,000 researchers worldwide bypass years of trial-and-error in designing next-generation materials. Dr. Huck holds a Ph.D. in High Energy Nuclear Physics and brings over 14 years of experience bridging the gap between complex research requirements and advanced computing infrastructure. Beyond his work on the Materials Project, he plays a key strategic role in the DOE’s newly launched Genesis Mission. As the L3 Co-Lead for the American Science Cloud’s Container Services & Platform, he is currently helping to design the unified, national-scale infrastructure that will connect the DOE’s most powerful supercomputers into a single, AI-ready ecosystem.

LLNL-VIDEO-2020324
channelDescription: For 70 years, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory has leveraged science and technology to make the world a safer place. Check out events, webinars, workshops and more that support the Lab's mission.

videoTitle: ZeroOps: Building AI-Powered No-Code DevOps on Google Cloud | Drag-n-Drop Infrastructure Automation
videoDescription:
Try It Out : https://zerops.tropicalcoders.xyz
ZeroOps is a no code DevOps automation and infrastructure orchestration platform that enables users to visually design server architectures through a drag and drop interface while abstracting the underlying operational complexity. The platform leverages advanced Google Cloud technologies including Google ADK and Gemini for intelligent requirement analysis architecture reasoning and automated infrastructure synthesis. Based on the user designed or agent generated architecture ZeroOps dynamically produces production grade Terraform code aligned with Google Cloud reference architectures and best practices. The system integrates deeply with core Google Cloud services such as Compute Engine GKE Cloud Run VPC Cloud Load Balancing Cloud IAM and Artifact Registry to enable secure scalable and highly available deployments. An agentic recommendation engine analyzes workload characteristics performance constraints and cost requirements to generate optimized architectures while a dedicated cost optimization agent applies resource right sizing pricing analysis and deployment efficiency strategies. ZeroOps also includes a cloud migration and correction engine capable of analyzing legacy or faulty Terraform configurations and refactoring them into optimized cloud ready infrastructure for target Google Cloud environments. The platform enforces security observability and compliance by default through native integration with Security Command Center Cloud Monitoring Cloud Logging and policy driven validation mechanisms delivering an end to end DevOps lifecycle covering design deployment optimization scaling and governance within a unified Google Cloud ecosystem.

videoTitle: Kubernetes Troubleshooting in Production: Real DevOps Debugging with Services, DNS & Netshoot
videoDescription:
In this hands-on Kubernetes troubleshooting session, we debug a realistic production-style deployment where everything looks healthy — Pods are running, Services are created, Deployments are green — yet the application does not work.

This lecture demonstrates real-world DevOps debugging techniques including:

Identifying Kubernetes Service port mismatches

Verifying traffic flow using Test-NetConnection / Telnet

Debugging from inside a Pod using kubectl exec

Understanding minimal container images

Using Netshoot for advanced network troubleshooting

Fixing cross-namespace DNS resolution issues

Testing database connectivity with Netcat (nc)

Differentiating ICMP vs TCP port checks

If you support Kubernetes in production, these troubleshooting skills are essential.

Perfect for: DevOps Engineers, SREs, Platform Engineers, Cloud Engineers, and Kubernetes administrators.

⏱️ Timestamps

00:00 – Introduction & System Overview
03:30 – Deploying the Challenge & Verifying Cluster State
07:00 – Service Connectivity Issue & Port Mismatch Debugging
10:30 – Application Loads but Database Connection Fails
14:00 – Debugging Inside the Pod & Missing Tools Problem
17:30 – Using Netshoot for DNS & Cross-Namespace Debugging
21:00 – Testing Database Port Connectivity with Netcat
24:30 – Understanding ICMP vs TCP & Structured Troubleshooting Approach

videoTitle: New 📚 Release! Architecting and Implementing DevOps for Infrastructure Management in Azure #books
videoDescription:
New 📚 Release! Architecting and Implementing DevOps for Infrastructure Management in Azure: Infrastructure DevOps in Azure: Configure, Deploy, & Automate Your Azure resources with terraform, GitHub Actions and Ansible by Ashish Raj

Embark on a transformative journey from basic to intricate infrastructure configurations—think high availability, scalability, and security—all through the power of Infrastructure as Code. Dive into the automation of Azure resource deployments, leveraging DevOps tools with comprehensive, step-by-step guidance and practical examples. But the learning doesn't stop at deployment; delve deeper into automated governance and monitoring, ensuring your infrastructure is not just built but also managed with precision and efficiency.

Find it on Leanpub!

#MicrosoftAzure #coding #programming #newreleases #books #bookstan

Link: https://leanpub.com/devopsinazure

About the Book

In the face of accelerated development cycles, Infrastructure teams must align with the culture of rapid deployment and infrastructure versioning. Embracing Infrastructure as Code, Continuous Integration, and Configuration Management practices becomes imperative. Tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, and Ansible empower infrastructure engineers to implement scalable Infrastructure as Code practices in Azure.

This book serves as a thorough guide for deploying, configuring, and automating Azure infrastructure using DevOps tools. It navigates through cloud architecture patterns with DevOps tools, illustrating their application in Azure solution design. Subsequently, the book delves into the implementation of the architected solution, utilizing DevOps tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, Packer, Ansible, etc., to address specific architectural requirements with emphasis on scaling the solution architecture and establishing governance, aligning with DevOps principles.

Upon finishing this book, readers will be equipped with the expertise to transform business requirements into Azure-compatible solution architectures, seamlessly incorporating a DevOps approach to deployment. Readers will demonstrate proficiency in executing end-to-end deployment with governance and monitoring proficiently through the consistent application of DevOps tools.

About the Author

Ashish Raj is a technologist and storyteller who helps engineering teams improve their velocity. He believes the biggest challenges facing engineers aren't technical, but not knowing how to tie people, process and technology. With an extensive background spanning innovative startups to leading tech conglomerates, Ashish currently serves as a Cloud & DevOps Architect at Haufe Group.

A fervent advocate and contributor to the DevOps and cloud communities, he shares expertise through webinars and local meetups. Holding Microsoft certifications in DevOps, Architecture, and Certified Microsoft Trainer. Ashish co-founded "AzureTalk," a prominent IM-based global Azure community on Telegram, and initiated "AzureDevOpsPro," a thriving DevOps community. He shares educational cloud and DevOps videos on his YouTube channel, youtube.com/ashishrajsrivastava

videoTitle: Master DevOps with PROMETHEUS Easily
videoDescription:
Are you new to DevOps and wondering what Prometheus actually does? In this short video, we break it down in a simple, beginner-friendly way.

Prometheus is a powerful open-source monitoring tool used by DevOps teams around the world. It pulls metrics from your servers, stores them as time-series data, and triggers alerts whenever thresholds are broken. This proactive approach helps teams detect issues before they impact users, improving uptime and system reliability.

Unlike log-based monitoring, Prometheus focuses on quantitative metrics like CPU usage, memory consumption, response times, and error rates. By monitoring these metrics continuously, DevOps engineers and SREs (Site Reliability Engineers) can maintain healthy systems, reduce downtime, and scale cloud-native infrastructure efficiently.

This video is perfect for beginners who want to understand Prometheus in under 20 seconds and start applying monitoring in real-world projects. Whether you’re managing corporate training platforms, retail applications, or cloud infrastructure, this knowledge is essential.

💡 Save this video and follow the channel for more quick DevOps tutorials, beginner tips, and monitoring hacks. Stay ahead in your career and make complex DevOps tools simple!

videoTitle: DevOps Hands-On | Ansible – Day 4  ( DevOpsFarm Day 46 )
videoDescription:
In this in-depth session, we dive into the critical world of DevOps monitoring and master the industry-leading open-source tools: Grafana, Prometheus, and Loki.

Learn why monitoring is essential for getting system feedback, taking proactive action, and preventing system failure due to issues like high CPU usage, low RAM, or disk space depletion. We cover the key aspects you must monitor, including system resources, logs, and traces.




What you will learn in this session:


The Importance of Monitoring: Understand the benefits, including anomaly detection, generating alerts, and creating useful dashboards for historical data analysis.



Core Monitoring Tools: An overview of popular tools like Grafana, New Relic, Datadog, and Dynatrace.



Prometheus Explained: A deep dive into Prometheus, the time-series database used for collecting and tracking metrics (like CPU usage over time).



Loki for Logging: Learn how Loki is used for log collection and parsing, making logs consumable by Grafana.



The Grafana Stack in Action: See how Grafana serves as the visualization layer, consuming data from Prometheus and Loki to create powerful dashboards and configure alerts.



Practical Demo: We demonstrate connecting data sources, exploring metrics using PromQL, and setting up label-based notification policies to ensure the right teams are alerted instantly.


Whether you are a beginner or a seasoned DevOps professional, this guide provides the knowledge and practical steps needed to implement robust monitoring for any application or microservice environment.

videoTitle: How to explain Grafana and Prometheus Stack deployment in Production in DevOps SRE interviews?
videoDescription:
Support my work: https://buymeacoffee.com/techapricate

In this video, I explain how to confidently describe the Grafana + Prometheus observability and monitoring stack deployment in production during DevOps and SRE interviews.

You’ll learn how Terraform modules stored in GitLab can be used to deploy the kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart for a complete Observability setup. I’ll walk you through how Prometheus collects application and cluster-level metrics from AWS EKS clusters, integrates with Prometheus Mimir for long-term storage, and how Grafana is configured with Mimir as a data source and PostgreSQL as a backend database.

This explanation will help you stand out in interviews by showing practical knowledge of real-world production-grade observability setups, from infrastructure as code (IaC) to monitoring architecture design.

🧠 What You’ll Learn:
How to explain Prometheus and Grafana observability architecture in interviews
How to use Terraform + GitLab for deploying kube-prometheus-stack
Integration of Prometheus with Mimir for scalable metric storage
How Grafana connects to Mimir as a data source and uses a PostgreSQL backend
How metrics are collected from EKS clusters and workloads via Kubernetes APIs

Best practices to explain Monitoring, Alerting, and Visualization in production

🚀 Technologies Covered:
Terraform | GitLab CI/CD | Prometheus | Grafana | Prometheus Mimir | PostgreSQL | kube-prometheus-stack | Helm | AWS EKS | Kubernetes Observability | Cloud Monitoring

videoTitle: DevOps Myths Everyone Gets Wrong!
videoDescription:
A lot of people think they understand DevOps but so many still get it wrong 😅.

In this short video, I’m breaking down the biggest DevOps misconceptions that confuse developers, IT teams, and tech leads everywhere.

If you’ve ever heard someone say “DevOps is just about tools” or “we’ll just hire a DevOps engineer and boom, we’re DevOps” — this one’s for you.

I’ll explain:
- Why DevOps isn’t about tools like Docker, Kubernetes, or Jenkins
- Why you can’t outsource DevOps culture
- And how DevOps connects developers and operations to work faster, smarter, and with fewer surprises.

Whether you’re new to DevOps or already deep in the cloud-native world, this short will help you finally understand what DevOps really is — and what it’s not.

👉 If you found this helpful, like the video, subscribe, and drop a comment telling me which DevOps myth you’ve heard the most.

videoTitle: Why DevOps transformations fail in regulated industries, with Merge Ready's Matt Bailey
videoDescription:
https://cortex.io/

00:00 Why DevOps is an outcome, not a toolchain.
02:47 How compliance can be automated instead of blocking delivery.
14:25 Automating GitLab CI/CD approvals through ServiceNow workflows.
22:08 Starting DevOps transformations with pilot teams and controls as code.
27:07 Why stakeholder management is a core DevOps skill.
32:43 How AI changes DevOps and the case for ClickOps.

Cortex co-founder and CTO Ganesh Datta sits down with Matt Bailey, DevOps consultant and founder of Merge Ready. Matt shares lessons from helping large regulated organizations in finance, healthcare, and government transform their DevOps practices, and explains why DevOps is an outcome rather than a toolchain.

Matt and Ganesh discuss why compliance can be mostly automated rather than a mandatory bottleneck, how to turn 30-day approval processes into continuous audit readiness through controls as code, and why treating platform teams as product teams drives natural adoption. They also explore decision latency as a core organizational problem, the importance of stakeholder management as a DevOps skill, and how AI agents may shift infrastructure drift management from prevention to embrace.

videoTitle: Is your multicloud pipeline compliant? DevOps vs GitOps
videoDescription:
DevOps Isn't Enough: GitOps Strategies for Regulated Multicloud Deployments

Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez walks through how GitOps practices and Argo CD make multicloud compliance manageable for regulated environments like FedRAMP, HIPAA, and Protected B. Learn how git becomes your audit trail, how evidence lockers automate proof of compliance, and how Argo CD's built-in RBAC, sync policies, and audit logging map to regulated deployment requirements across AWS, IBM, and Google Cloud.

Summary: 
Your project's direct dependencies are the easy part. Viral Chhasatia walks through dependency auditing across Python, npm, Maven, Go, Rust, and Docker using tools like pip licenses, CycloneDX, npm license checker, go licenses, cargo about, and Sift. Learn why transitive dependencies carry hidden license risks, why your SBOM is already outdated, and why no single tool gives you the complete picture.

Session: Bridging DevOps And GitOps: MultiCloud Strategies For HyperScalers

GitOps has transformed the way organizations manage cloud-native applications, but scaling it across federal and commercial hyperscalers presents unique challenges. In this session, Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez will share lessons learned from leading GitOps implementations in highly regulated environments, covering best practices for security, compliance, automation, and scalability. Attendees will gain insights into key differences between federal and commercial cloud GitOps workflows, overcoming security and compliance hurdles in highly regulated environments, enhancing CI/CD pipelines for resilience, observability, and automation, and lessons from real-world implementations, including multi-cloud strategies and open source technologies.



Learn more about GitOps strategies for complex multi-cloud deployment environments. Sujeily Fonseca-Gonzalez explains how to manage infrastructure consistency across hyperscalers.

This session breaks down the technical transition from DevOps to GitOps, specifically addressing the challenges of tool fragmentation and security in distributed systems. If you are an engineer or architect managing infrastructure, this overview provides clarity on maintaining compliance while scaling operations across different platforms.

Learn to overcome common hurdles when implementing GitOps strategies at scale. The discussion covers practical lessons from real-world multi-cloud deployment scenarios, focusing on how to maintain security standards and operational consistency. By the end, you will understand the necessary architectural shifts required for successful cloud infrastructure management.

#GitOps #ArgoCD #MultiCloud #Kubernetes #DevOps #Compliance #FedRAMP #AllThingsOpen

videoTitle: 20260325 Building a DevOps Pipeline with Cloud Build Trigger on Google Cloud Platform
videoDescription:
👉 This demo walks through how to build a DevOps pipeline on Google Cloud Platform using a Cloud Build trigger that automatically runs whenever code is pushed to a GitHub repository. When triggered, it deploys a simple Flask application to a VM instance, where users can access a “Hello Build Trigger” webpage over HTTP or HTTPS. The entire workflow is defined in the cloudbuild.yaml file.

Step 1. The pipeline begins by detecting the active project and determining the allowed region and zones, ensuring that all resources are created in the correct and permitted environment.

Step 2. It then builds a Docker image for the Flask application using the source code from the repository, preparing it for deployment.

Step 3. Next, the pipeline either creates a new VM instance or updates an existing one. It installs Docker, Nginx, and OpenSSL, runs the Docker container for the Flask app, generates a self-signed SSL certificate, and configures Nginx as a reverse proxy to handle HTTPS traffic. If the VM already exists, it skips the setup and simply redeploys the updated image.

Step 4. Finally, it sets up firewall rules to allow incoming HTTP and HTTPS traffic, making the application accessible from the internet.

✅ GCP DevOps Pipeline Demo – Written Guide
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nIz91opc7jwgZ_yi3YOq8nS45-WG25lwb2WHZ2I_-zY
✅ GitHub repo
https://github.com/nov05/gcp-devops-repo-example/

videoTitle: InFocus - Honeycomb: Confidence to Code with AI #LaunchDarkly #FeatureFlags #DevOps #AIDevelopment
videoDescription:
Code velocity didn’t just increase. It multiplied.

AI is pushing teams to ship faster than ever. But that speed changes everything—how we review code, how teams collaborate, and how we trust what goes to production.

Now the question isn’t just “can we ship this?” It’s “can we control it if something goes wrong?”

That’s where observability and feature flags come in. With LaunchDarkly, teams can ship fast, monitor impact in real time, and take immediate action—no redeploys, no guesswork.
channelDescription:
LaunchDarkly is the runtime control layer for the AI era. For over a decade we have helped engineering teams control what ships in production, from feature flags to full release governance. Today that mission extends to the AI agents and code that are increasingly building, modifying, and operating software on behalf of engineering teams.

With CodeControl and AgentControl, teams can ship faster, govern what runs in production, and automatically respond when something needs to change, without waiting on a redeploy. LaunchDarkly runs on infrastructure that handles over 50 trillion flag evaluations a day, which means the same platform that governs your features also governs your agents.

Founded in 2014 by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal, LaunchDarkly has been named on the Forbes Cloud 100 list and serves some of the most demanding engineering teams in the world.

Learn more at launchdarkly.com

videoTitle: TOGAF ADM Phases A-E: My Real DevOps Transformation
videoDescription:
The Transformation Challenge:
We transitioned from chaotic bi-weekly deployments to autonomous teams with same-day releases, showing that effective "software development" practices don't require full adherence to complex frameworks. This video focuses on how leveraging practical aspects of "devops" and "continuous delivery" helped us overcome significant coordination challenges. We also explore the mistakes made, demonstrating how to avoid "project failure" and ensure "project success" through careful "project management" and adaptation.

What You'll Learn:
• The Phase Structure Rule: Why transformation without phase structure becomes chaotic firefighting instead of systematic capability building
• The Translation Rule: How a 6-page vision document (versus 47 pages nobody read) eliminated weeks of stakeholder debates through diplomatic translation
• The Ownership Boundary Rule: The framework that reduced coordination meetings by 80% and ended "shared responsibility" paralysis
• Real artifacts from the transformation: 6-page vision documents, API contract examples, domain boundary frameworks
• Selective TOGAF implementation: How we used only Phases A-E (not the full cycle) to match organizational constraints
• Three micro-recaps with actionable "what you can copy" frameworks for immediate application

Chapters:
00:23 The Impossible Request - DevOps transformation challenge
02:14 Vision or Chaos - Phase A Architecture Vision reality check
04:12 Domain Wars - Phases B & C Business and Information Architecture battles
06:32 Platform Revolution - Phases D & E Technology and Implementation Planning
08:32 Framework Success - Transformation outcomes and three hard-won insights

The Real Story:
This case study reveals what actually worked in a 12-month government DevOps transformation where systematic phase application converted platform team bottlenecks into self-service enablement. You'll see the mistakes that nearly killed the project (like 47-page documents nobody read), the diplomatic battles with competing stakeholders, and the three named rules that emerged from applying TOGAF methodology to real organizational chaos.

Each scene includes practical micro-recaps with three elements: what we did, why it mattered, and what you can copy for your own transformation challenges.

Key Frameworks Covered:
- TOGAF ADM (Architecture Development Method) Phases A through E
- Phase A: Architecture Vision with stakeholder translation
- Phase B: Business Architecture with domain ownership boundaries
- Phase C: Information Systems Architecture with API contract design
- Phase D: Technology Architecture transforming platform teams
- Phase E: Opportunities and Solutions with systematic migration planning

Important Disclaimers:
Personal experience sharing from one government transformation context - your organizational dynamics, cultural constraints, and stakeholder politics will differ significantly. This is not career coaching, consulting advice, or universal transformation solutions. Framework examples provided for reflection and systematic adaptation to your specific environment. Your mileage may vary.

Related Blog:
- https://www.janisexplainsarchitecture.com/blog/career-recognition-development/topic-002-togaf-adm-in-practice-how-phases-a-e-saved-our-devops-transformation-from-stakeholder-chaos.html

- Email: youtube.contact@janisexplainsarchitecture.com
- Website: https://www.janisexplainsarchitecture.com/

videoTitle: Power BI IT - WK08 - DevOps... or not?
videoDescription:
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the origin of DevOps, a technical, peer-reviewed, managed way of working. It is built to ensure technical solutions are delivered using tools like GitHub, Bitbucket or one of a multitude of others. The core element is that the review is technical, focusing on "Is the code effective and efficient?" This makes it ideal for specific workloads and elements. 

Power BI Deployment Pipelines are designed to span multiple workloads and promote between environments. It is technically possible to cherry-pick and promote only specific elements across environments; however, the question remains: "Is that DevOps?" Manually cherry-picking risk human error. DevOps demands that our tooling merge changes so that the codebase remains the codebase.

So, Power BI Deployment Pipelines are (in our view) not DevOps, they are instead artefacts of the more traditional ITIL-based way of working. There is no inherent issue with either, but they are not necessarily the same.

Mixing methodologies within your organisation is not a problem, but each should have a specific, well-defined focus. In our demo, we used DevOps to build the development environment of a Deployment Pipeline. Hopefully, you agree with us that this is less than optimal. However, using DevOps to build the Data Engineering activities (unfortunately, we cannot really demo that as there is no compatible Pro or PPU compatible option). So, at present, direct DevOps integration in Data Engineering may be the gift of Fabric only.

IaC for Semantic Model Development is more problematic, as the likelihood that both technical and less-technical resources need to be involved can make a true DevOps implementation challenging, since reconciling Power BI Desktop Development with GitHub is not ideal. 

Our advice would be that DevOps is a great option for Data Engineering workloads, but as soon as Data Analysts need to be involved, a more traditional process is more appropriate.

videoTitle: Who Should Learn Prometheus? | DevOps Career Tips
videoDescription:
Are you wondering if learning Prometheus is worth your time? The answer is YES—if you are pursuing a career in DevOps, SRE, Cloud Engineering, or Backend Development.

Prometheus is no longer just a “nice-to-have” tool—it has become essential for modern infrastructure monitoring. Companies rely on Prometheus to track system metrics, detect anomalies, and trigger alerts before users are impacted. Whether you manage servers, cloud applications, or Kubernetes clusters, understanding Prometheus is critical to maintaining high uptime and performance.

For DevOps engineers, Site Reliability Engineers (SREs), cloud architects, and backend developers, Prometheus is a career-boosting skill. It empowers teams to move from reactive problem-solving to proactive monitoring, ensuring that systems run smoothly and efficiently. In addition, many organizations integrate Prometheus with tools like Grafana for dashboards and visualization, making your monitoring workflow even more powerful.

Learning Prometheus opens doors to high-demand roles in IT, cloud computing, and enterprise DevOps. This short video explains who should learn Prometheus and why—all in under 15 seconds.

💡 Follow this channel for more career-focused tech tips, DevOps tutorials, and beginner-friendly guides that help you master tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and cloud monitoring.

videoTitle: AI Is Changing What DevOps Engineers Do
videoDescription:
For years, infrastructure teams rewarded people who knew the right commands.

AI can now analyze logs, correlate alerts, summarize incidents, detect anomalies, and even suggest remediation steps.

As a result, the value of an engineer is shifting toward system design, reliability, automation, observability, security, and architecture.

The question is no longer who can respond fastest.

It's who can build systems that need less human intervention.

Topics: AI, DevOps, SRE, Platform Engineering, Cloud Infrastructure, Automation, AIOps, Observability, System Design.
channelDescription:
Systems rarely fail where the metrics say they should.

SystemAxis investigates real infrastructure failures: servers that collapse under load, performance that degrades without obvious cause, and configurations that behave differently in production than in demos.

Each video starts with a failure — dropped connections, misleading CPU usage, scaling that makes performance worse — and follows the investigation using Linux tools, load tests, and controlled experiments until the real system limit is uncovered.

The goal is not tutorials. The goal is to expose the hidden mechanics of production systems.

No hype. No demo-only advice. Just production reality.

videoTitle: 100 Days of DevOps – Day 1: Linux User Setup with Non‑Interactive Shell
videoDescription:
🚀 100 Days of DevOps – Day 1: Linux User Setup with Non-Interactive Shell

🎓 Join the Challenge (Free)  
Start here: https://J8EQo9.short.gy/devops-challenge

In this video, I walk through Day 1 of KodeKloud’s 100 Days of DevOps challenge: creating a Linux user with a non‑interactive shell for a backup agent on App Server 1 at xFusionCorp Industries.

🧩 Lab Scenario (KodeKloud Engineer) 
To meet the backup agent tool’s requirements, the system admin team at xFusionCorp needs a dedicated service user that cannot log in interactively.

📌 Task for Day 1
• Create a user with a non interactive shell on App Server 1
• Use the infrastructure details provided in the “Details of all Users and Servers” section in the lab UI

🔍 What You’ll Learn
• Interactive vs non interactive shells in Linux
• How and why to use shells like /sbin/nologin or /bin/false for service accounts
• The exact steps to SSH into the right server and create a restricted user for a production style environment

🎯 Why This Matters for DevOps 
Service users with non‑interactive shells are a core security best practice: they let tools and daemons run safely without exposing an interactive login for humans or attackers. This is a foundational skill you’ll reuse across CI/CD pipelines, automation, and real‑world Linux administration.

👍 Like, subscribe, and follow along as I complete all 100 days of DevOps labs, one hands on task at a time!

videoTitle: How Top Teams Monitor Infrastructure in 2026 #DevOps #Grafana
videoDescription:
Prometheus is powerful—but Prometheus alone isn’t enough. In modern DevOps and cloud environments, collecting and storing metrics is only half the job. 

You also need clear, real-time visualization to fully understand what’s happening inside your complex systems. That’s where the Prometheus + Grafana combo becomes absolutely essential for any team aiming for efficiency. 

Prometheus is responsible for collecting and storing crucial system metrics such as CPU usage, memory consumption, response time, and error rates. It works efficiently using time-series data and triggers alerts when specific thresholds are exceeded, ensuring that you can act quickly when issues arise. 

However, raw metrics alone can be challenging and difficult to interpret at scale, especially in large environments. This is where Grafana steps in to solve this problem by transforming Prometheus metrics into beautiful, interactive real-time dashboards that are easy to read and understand. 

With Grafana, DevOps teams can visualize trends, detect anomalies faster, and monitor the overall health of their infrastructure at a glance, making it far easier to manage resources. 

This powerful combination is widely used in cloud-native applications, Kubernetes orchestration, and enterprise-level DevOps environments across various industries. 

Together, Prometheus and Grafana help teams move from reactive troubleshooting to proactive monitoring and observability, making it easier to identify potential issues before they escalate. 

That’s precisely why this stack is a standard choice for a wide range of professionals, including DevOps engineers, Site Reliability Engineers (SREs), and cloud architects who are on the front lines of technology. If you’re learning DevOps or currently working with monitoring tools, understanding how Prometheus and Grafana work together is an invaluable must-have skill that can greatly enhance your capabilities. 

👉 Comment “Grafana” if you want a full video explaining dashboards, metrics, and real-world setups, including tips and best practices. 

👉 Follow for more bite-sized DevOps and monitoring content that can help you stay up to date in this fast-evolving field. 

Ref: https://prometheus.io/

videoTitle: Explain CI/CD Like I'm 5 (DevOps Interview Answer) 🏭
videoDescription:
Can you explain CI/CD to a 5-year-old? 🏭 If you are preparing for DevOps interview questions and answers, this is the one concept you cannot afford to get wrong. Many candidates fail by using complex jargon like "integration" and "deployment" without actually understanding the workflow.

In this video, I break down the CI/CD pipeline using a simple factory analogy that recruiters love. We explain how Continuous Integration (CI) acts like an assembly line for your code, checking for errors automatically. Then, we look at how Continuous Delivery (CD) serves as the delivery truck that ships your software to customers instantly. This explanation works perfectly for both IT and Non-IT professionals looking to break into the Cloud industry.

Whether you are studying for your next AWS certification, Azure Architect exam, or just looking for practical DevOps tips, mastering these basics is crucial. We cover the difference between CI and CD, why automation matters, and how to present this answer confidently to hiring managers.

videoTitle: What is Platform Engineering? (The Death of Traditional DevOps?)
videoDescription:
Is your team drowning in infrastructure complexity? 🌊
In this tutorial, we dive deep into Platform Engineering, the discipline that is officially taking over the cloud-native era. By 2026, Gartner predicts that 80% of engineering organizations will have dedicated platform teams
.
What you will learn in this video:
The "Why": How to solve the crisis of cognitive overload where developers spend up to 42% of their time on maintenance rather than building features
.
The "How": Designing Internal Developer Platforms (IDPs) that provide "Golden Paths" (paved roads)—standardized, secure, and scalable self-service capabilities that guide developers to success without manual hand-holding
.
The Tools: An overview of the ultimate platform stack, including Backstage (Spotify’s open-source framework), Kubernetes, Terraform, and AWS CodePipeline
.
The Business Value: Why treating your Platform as a Product is the key to reducing lead times by up to 62% and increasing deployment frequency

videoTitle: インフラ作業を生成 AI にどこまで任せるか【20260710 AI×DevOpsStudy #18】
videoDescription:
## AI×DevOps Study #18の概要
2026年7月10日に開催した「AI×DevOps Study」第18回のアーカイブ動画です。

「AI×DevOps Study」は、AI駆動開発やそこに関係するマイクロサービスについて理解を深める場になります。
株式会社ScalarではAIを使ったチーム開発を進めており、参画しているメンバーや協力会社の方から、具体的なAI駆動開発を実施する方法、その中で生まれたマイクロサービスアーキテクチャを使用したAI駆動開発の事例や実際に使えるエージェントについてお話頂き、参加者の皆様と知識の共有や交換を目的としています。
（弊社製品であるScalarDBも絡んだお話も一部出てきますが、汎用的な内容となっておりますのでフラットにお楽しみいいただけます）

## 今回のテーマ 
「インフラ作業を生成 AI にどこまで任せるか」

生成 AI でコードを書く話は増えましたが、「インフラを生成 AI でどこまで任せていいのか」は、まだ手探りの人が多いのではないでしょうか。

本 LT では、実際の IaC リポジトリで Claude をどう使っているかを、設定・スキル・運用そのままに共有します。設計書づくりや実装をどう任せ、Terraform や Helm のリソース差異をどうレビューし、devcontainer による再現環境や CLAUDE.md・Skill でチーム知識をどう固定化しているか。また、API Gateway の構成変更を通じて、生成 AI をどのように使ったかの実例を紹介します。目的・差異・方針から設計・実装までをタスクガイドに整理して段階的に進めました。設計と実装を生成 AI に任せつつ、人間が差分を確認しながら破壊的変更を伴うクラウド環境への適用を実施しています。ここまでの issue 駆動・差分レビュー・Skill を組み合わせると、こういう検証がどう回るのか、をお見せします。

誤っても回収できる作業から任せ、危ない操作は人間が承認する という、現場で回している線引きの話が中心です。生成 AI をインフラ作業に持ち込みたい方に、明日から試せる具体を持ち帰ってもらえる内容にします。

こんな人におすすめ

・インフラで生成 AI を使い始めたい / 使い方を見直したい方
・Claude Code や devcontainer を実務でどう構成しているか知りたい方
・生成 AI を使って、破壊的変更を伴う移行をどう安全に進めるか知りたい方

話すこと

・IaC と生成 AI が相性のいい理由と、適用しやすい / 慎重に扱う領域
・設計書作成・実装・差分レビューの実際の回し方
・devcontainer / Skill / Hook / MCP による環境とチーム知識の固定化
・API Gateway の構成変更を通じて、issue 駆動・レビュー・Skill でどう進めたか

## 登壇者情報（敬称略）
### 佐々木 亮輔
株式会社電通総研に在籍。現在は Azure・Kubernetes・Terraform を中心としたクラウドインフラ開発のテックリードとして、アーキテクチャ設計や IaC による基盤構築を推進。これまでに Hadoop/Spark を活用したビッグデータ基盤のインフラ構築や、Golang を用いたバックエンド開発のアーキテクチャ設計に携わる。

## AI×DevOps Study は定期開催しています。

videoTitle: DevOps vs. Platform Engineering: What’s Actually Changing in 2026?
videoDescription:
In this tutorial, we dive deep into the most significant structural shift in software delivery for 2026: the transition from pure DevOps to Platform Engineering.
While DevOps changed how we build software, Platform Engineering is changing how that change scales
. As engineering environments grow more complex with microservices, multi-cloud, and AI, developers are hitting a "breaking point" of cognitive load
.
In this video, you will learn:
The Category Error: Why DevOps is a culture and Platform Engineering is its implementation at scale
.
The Internal Developer Platform (IDP): How to build a curated, self-service layer that abstracts infrastructure complexity
.
Golden Paths vs. Paved Roads: How to create "opinionated" workflows that encode security and compliance by default
.
Shift Left vs. Shift Down: Moving beyond "you build it, you run it" to a model where developers consume infrastructure as a product
.
The 2026 Outlook: Why Gartner predicts 80% of large organizations will have dedicated platform teams by the end of this year
.
The AI Impact: How platform maturity has become a prerequisite for successfully deploying AI coding assistants and agentic workflows
.
Whether you are a DevOps Engineer, SRE, or Software Developer, understanding this evolution is critical for your career in 2026
.

videoTitle: Microsoft ADO & Tosca OnPrem Integration
videoDescription:
This demonstration shows how Conigma Connect integrates Microsoft Azure DevOps with Tricentis Tosca On-Premises to connect business requirements, test management, and test automation within an SAP-focused environment.

Business requirements captured as User Stories in Azure DevOps can be linked to reusable Tosca test assets. Conigma Connect synchronizes the relevant test structures between both systems, enabling test managers to plan tests in Azure DevOps while using Tosca for automated execution. Selected test cases are automatically transferred to Tosca and organized for execution.

After execution, Conigma Connect synchronizes execution results back to Azure DevOps, providing end-to-end traceability from requirements to test outcomes. In combination with Conigma CCM, transport and change management information can also be integrated into the process.

Key benefits include reduced manual effort, improved traceability, centralized visibility of testing activities, faster feedback cycles, and stronger alignment between business requirements and automated testing.
channelDescription:

The Conigma™ software suite also includes solutions for the integration of SAP and third-party tools in the IT factory, recovery from SAP system copies, synchronisation of parallel SAP development systems and integration of functionalities in ABAP in Eclipse.

The Galileo Group is a partner of SAP and Microsoft. The owner-managed company, founded in 2000, has a branch office in Switzerland in addition to its headquarters in Munich.

We'll explore why training consumes massive GPU resources, why inference dominates production, and why this distinction is essential for DevOps Engineers, Platform Engineers, and MLOps practitioners.

videoTitle: 10. What is DevOps? Explained in 60 Seconds #devops #software #techexplained #codingforbeginners
videoDescription:
#devops #software #techexplained #codingforbeginners 

What is DevOps?

DevOps is a cultural philosophy and set of practices that unites software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops). Its goal is to automate and streamline the software lifecycle, allowing teams to build, test, and release secure, high-quality software much faster and more reliably than traditional methods. 

Why DevOps Matters 
Traditionally, software developers and IT operations teams worked in isolated "silos." Developers would write code and throw it over the wall to the operations team to deploy, often resulting in miscommunication, delays, and system instability. DevOps bridges this gap through shared responsibility, automation, and continuous feedback. 

Core Practices: 
CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery): A practice where code changes are automatically built, tested, and prepped for release. It ensures bugs are caught early and updates can be shipped in small, frequent batches. 

Infrastructure as Code (IaC): 
Managing and provisioning computing infrastructure (servers, networks) through code and configuration files rather than manually setting up physical hardware.

Automated Testing: 
Running scripts to test software performance and security before it goes live, reducing deployment risks.

Monitoring and Logging: 
Continuously tracking software performance in real-time once it is live, which provides rapid feedback to fix errors quickly and improve future features.

videoTitle: AgentCon26 Clariza Look - Beyond the Prototype: DevOps Thinking for Agentic AI in Production
videoDescription:
AI agents are quickly moving from experiments and demos into real-world applications. But building an agent is only half the challenge, operating it reliably as part of a production system is where the real complexity begins.

In this session, we explore how DevOps thinking can help bridge the gap between AI prototypes and production-ready systems. Drawing from real-world cloud and infrastructure experience, we'll look at how principles such as infrastructure as code, observability, automation, and continuous delivery apply to agentic AI applications.

Through a practical architecture example, a student admission web app powered by an AI agent built with .NET and running in containers, we'll examine how the agent interacts with APIs, tools, and external systems to drive admission workflows. We'll look at what engineers need to consider when deploying, scaling, and monitoring these AI-powered systems in production environments.

Rather than focusing on model training or prompt tricks, this talk focuses on the systems thinking required to run AI agents reliably: how they are deployed, how their decisions are observed, and how failures are handled in real-world environments.

Attendees will walk away with practical insights and architecture patterns for moving from AI experiments to production-ready agentic systems.
